narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 61.7% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from how your profile stacks up against patterns in students who typically pass or fail the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to peers who succeed on the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength from the start. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income stood out as the third key factor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since students from similar backgrounds often fare better, perhaps due to added support resources. While your lower-than-average LSAT and GPA played roles, these decile rankings carried the heaviest weight in tipping the scales. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in higher deciles and score around 36.7 on the LSAT with a 3.21 GPA, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes these bar passage signals for admissions fairness. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value diverse backgrounds like yours. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
